title: RecoveryCodesDownload
description: DTO returned by POST /api/v1/me/mfa/recovery-codes/regenerate (fresh set) OR POST /api/v1/me/mfa/recovery-codes/download (re-emit current set). Emits plaintext recovery codes ONCE. Not persisted anywhere retrievable — the caller must save the codes client-side or they are lost. Regeneration purges the previous bcrypt-hashed array + writes 10 fresh hashes; download re-emits the CURRENT unconsumed set without invalidating anything.

x-storage.note: The plaintext codes are generated in-memory during the request, emitted in the response, and NEVER cached / logged / persisted. Only the bcrypt hashes are persisted on the Identity row (owned by identity/identity).

codes.description: Array of 10 plaintext recovery codes. Each code is 10 characters from a Crockford base32 alphabet (0-9 + A-Z minus I / L / O / U). Approximately 50 bits of entropy per code. Rendered client-side as either a raw grid or with the group separator (`-` every 5 characters) for readability. The separator is presentational only — the server strips it before hash / compare.

generated_at.description: When the code array was generated (or last regenerated). For a download response, this is the timestamp of the last regenerate — the codes may have been generated hours or days ago; download just re-emits the current set.

expires_at.description: Always null. Recovery codes never expire — regeneration is the only invalidation path. Included in the schema explicitly to signal the invariant.

trigger.description: Which endpoint produced this response. Downstream analytics + audit distinguish the two — a regenerate is a rare-but-normal event; a download is a data-egress event that is audit-logged with warn severity.

regenerate_url.description: Signed URL back to POST /me/mfa/recovery-codes/regenerate. Included so a client that receives the download response with a low code count can offer a one-click regenerate CTA. Signed with a 5-minute expiry so the URL can't be scraped from logs + replayed.

x-wire.cache_control_reason: Response carries plaintext secrets. Explicit `Cache-Control: no-store, private, max-age=0` header + `Pragma: no-cache` header for legacy proxies.

invariants: The plaintext codes exist in the caller's browser (rendered in the response body) + the server's memory during the request. They exist NOWHERE ELSE. The Identity row stores only bcrypt hashes.

Consuming a code (via POST /me/mfa/challenge/{id}/verify with method=recovery_code) removes the matching hash from the Identity's JSONB array in the same transaction as the login. Once removed, the plaintext code is unusable — subsequent submissions return CHALLENGE_INVALID_RECOVERY_CODE.

The download endpoint (as distinct from regenerate) returns the CURRENT unconsumed set — codes that have already been consumed do NOT appear. If the array is empty (all consumed), download returns MFA_RECOVERY_CODES_EMPTY (409) + instructs the caller to regenerate.
Both endpoints require a recent MFA challenge (`mfa.require_recent_challenge` middleware). A stolen session cookie cannot download the codes without proving current possession of the primary factor.

Regeneration is an atomic operation — the old array is purged and the fresh array is written in the same transaction. There is no observable state where the caller's Identity has zero codes as an intermediate.
